title: The First Death
content:
The forum thread was seven years old and buried under seventeen layers of spam.

Zhang Xia found it at 3:47 AM, hunched over her laptop in a dorm room that smelled like instant noodles and desperation. Her roommate had gone home for the winter break. The heating had died three hours ago. She'd wrapped herself in two blankets and a hoodie that had once been black but was now the color of regret.

She'd been searching for answers about her mother's death. Instead, she found a link that would kill her.

The thread title was in broken English: "REAL past life app. Not fake. I try it."

Below it, seven years of replies. Most were variations of "scam" and "OP is crazy" and "where download". The original poster never responded. But in the middle of the thread, buried under a cascade of troll comments, someone had posted a single line:

"APK unlocked. 24 hours only. Last chance before they patch it."

No name. No avatar. Just a link.

Zhang Xia stared at it. Her fingers were numb from cold. Her eyes burned from three nights of caffeine and grief. She should have been sleeping. She should have been processing. Instead, she was scrolling through the dark web, looking for ghosts.

She clicked.

The download took six seconds. The file was small. Smaller than any app she'd ever seen. No name. No icon. Just a blank square that installed itself onto her phone without asking permission.

She almost deleted it. Her thumb hovered over the uninstall button.

Then the app opened.

Black screen. White text. One word:

SAMSARA

Below it, a single line of instruction:

*"To enter a past life, hold the phone and close your eyes."*

Zhang Xia laughed. A hollow, exhausted sound. This was stupid. This was a virus. This was exactly the kind of thing her mother had warned her about.

But her mother was dead.

And what else was she going to do at four in the morning?

She held the phone. She closed her eyes.

Nothing happened.

She opened her eyes. The screen was still black. Still white text. Still the same instruction.

She tried again. Held the phone tighter. Squeezed her eyes shut until she saw colors behind her eyelids.

Nothing.

"Stupid," she muttered. She tossed the phone onto her bed and reached for her laptop.

But her hand never made it.

The world twisted.

It wasn't a feeling of falling. It wasn't spinning. It was more like someone had grabbed the fabric of reality and pulled it sideways. Zhang Xia felt her body stretch, compress, dissolve. She tried to scream, but she had no mouth. She tried to open her eyes, but she had no eyes.

Then she had a body again.

It was not her body.

She was kneeling on cold stone. Rough fabric bound her wrists behind her back. The air smelled of incense and blood. Torches flickered along stone walls, casting shadows that danced like living things.

Zhang Xia tried to move. Her legs wouldn't respond. Her arms were numb. Her head hung forward, heavy with ornaments—hairpins, flowers, gold chains that clinked when she breathed.

"Rise," a voice said.

She looked up.

A man stood before her. He wore robes of deep purple, embroidered with golden dragons. His face was smooth, ageless, carved from jade and cruelty. Behind him, soldiers in armor stood at attention. Their swords were drawn.

Zhang Xia tried to speak, but her voice came out as a whisper. "Where am I?"

The man smiled. It was not a kind smile. "The Forbidden City, naturally. Did you think you could poison the Emperor's tea and walk away?"

Poison. Emperor. Forbidden City.

The words hit her like a physical blow. She looked down at her hands. They were pale, elegant, covered in silk. Rings adorned every finger. She wore robes of green and gold, rich fabrics that whispered of power.

A concubine. She was a concubine.

And she was about to be executed.

Memory flooded in—not her memory, but someone else's. A woman named Lin Yue. Nineteen years old. Favorite of the Emperor. Accused of treason by the Empress Dowager. The evidence was planted. The trial was a formality. The execution was scheduled for dawn.

Zhang Xia felt the woman's emotions like echoes. Fear. Rage. Despair.

But beneath them, something else. Something sharp and cold.

*She isn't me*, Zhang Xia thought. *She's dead already. I'm just wearing her skin.*

The executioner stepped forward. He was a massive man, bare-chested despite the cold, a curved blade gleaming in his hand.

"The Emperor has granted you one final request," the official said. "Speak it, and it shall be honored."

Zhang Xia's mind raced. She knew this period. Tang Dynasty. Emperor Xuanzong's reign, before the An Lushan Rebellion. She'd written a paper on it last semester. She'd read the primary sources, the poetry, the court records.

She knew what happened to concubines who were accused of treason.

They were beheaded.

"Water," she said. Her voice came out steady. "I want water."

The official raised an eyebrow. "That is your request?"

"No." Zhang Xia met his eyes. "I want to write a poem first. As is my right as a scholar of the court."

The official's expression flickered. Concubines were not scholars. But Lin Yue had been educated. She had been favored. And in the Tang Dynasty, a dying person's poetic request was sacred.

The official nodded. "Bring ink and paper."

The soldiers brought her a low table, inkstone, brush. They untied her hands. Her fingers trembled as she picked up the brush. But her mind was clear.

She wasn't going to write a poem.

She was going to write a chemical formula.

Gunpowder.

The recipe was simple. Saltpeter, sulfur, charcoal. She'd memorized it from a YouTube video about ancient Chinese warfare. The proportions were 75:10:15. The saltpeter was used in food preservation. The sulfur was available in the palace's alchemy chambers. The charcoal was everywhere.

But she didn't need to make gunpowder.

She just needed to make them think she could.

Zhang Xia dipped the brush. She wrote in careful, elegant characters:

*"Saltpeter from the kitchens. Sulfur from the alchemists. Charcoal from the fires. Mix in proportion. Add to the Empress's chambers. Light. Watch."*

She signed it with Lin Yue's name.

Then she looked up at the official. "Give this to the Empress Dowager. Tell her I have discovered a weapon that will destroy her enemies. She will want to see me alive."

The official's eyes narrowed. "You expect me to deliver a threat to the Empress Dowager?"

"I expect you to deliver a solution," Zhang Xia said. "The Tibetans are at the border. The generals are restless. The Empire needs new weapons. I am offering one."

She was bluffing. She had no idea if gunpowder existed yet in this era. It might. It might not. But she knew one thing: the Tang Dynasty was obsessed with alchemy. They believed in elixirs, in transformations, in the power of chemical reactions.

And the Empress Dowager was paranoid.

The official took the paper. He read it. His expression didn't change.

"Wait here," he said.

He left.

The executioner stayed. His blade glinted in the torchlight.

Zhang Xia watched the official's back disappear into the darkness. Her heart pounded so hard she could hear it in her ears. She had maybe five minutes. Maybe ten. If the Empress Dowager didn't buy it, she'd be dead before dawn.

But that wasn't the real problem.

The real problem was that she had no idea how to get back to her own body.

She looked down at her phone. It was still in her hand. The screen was cracked—no, not cracked. It was fading. The edges of the phone were becoming translucent, like smoke.

She held it tighter.

*Wake up*, she thought. *Wake up wake up wake up.*

Nothing.

The executioner shifted his weight. "The poem was clever," he said. His voice was low, almost kind. "But she won't believe you."

Zhang Xia looked at him. "Who?"

"The Empress Dowager." He shook his head. "She's already signed the death warrant. Nothing you say will change that."

"Then why haven't you killed me yet?"

The executioner smiled. It was a sad smile, full of years and blood. "Because I like poetry."

Footsteps echoed from the corridor. Multiple sets. Coming fast.

The executioner's smile faded. "She's coming herself. That's not good."

Zhang Xia's phone flickered. The screen went black. Then white. Then black again.

A notification appeared:

*"Samsara connection unstable. Remain calm. Do not resist."*

Remain calm. Right.

The Empress Dowager swept into the chamber. She was old, but not frail. Her robes were black and red, the colors of power. Her eyes were sharp, predatory, missing nothing.

She held the paper in her hand.

"This is nonsense," she said. "You are a concubine. You know nothing of weapons."

"Then burn it," Zhang Xia said. "And miss your chance."

The Empress Dowager's eyes flickered. She was calculating. Weighing. Deciding.

And in that moment of hesitation, Zhang Xia's phone buzzed.

The sound was loud in the silent chamber. Everyone stared.

The Empress Dowager's expression shifted from curiosity to rage. "What is that?"

Zhang Xia looked at the phone. The screen was flashing:

*"Connection restored. Returning to present."*

"Sorry," she said. "I have to go."

The world twisted again.

She felt herself falling, dissolving, stretching. The last thing she saw was the Empress Dowager's face, frozen in shock. The last thing she heard was the executioner's laugh.

Then she was back in her dorm room.

The heater was still broken. The instant noodles were still cold. Her laptop screen still glowed with the dark web forum.

And her phone was lying on the bed, screen dark, silent.

She picked it up.

The app was gone.

But there was a new notification in her settings:

*"Past life completed. Rewards pending. Next jump available in 24 hours."*

Zhang Xia stared at the screen.

Below the notification, in smaller text:

*"Warning: Changes to the past may affect the present. Proceed with caution."*

She looked at her hands. They were her hands again. Short nails. Cheap rings. A small scar on her thumb from a cooking accident.

But something was different.

There was dirt under her fingernails. Red dirt. The color of Tang Dynasty soil.

And on her wrist, hidden beneath her sleeve, a bruise in the shape of a hand.

The executioner's hand.

The past wasn't past.

And she was already marked.

title: The First Jump
content:
The notification appeared at 3:47 AM.

Zhang Xia's phone buzzed against the wooden desk in her dorm, the sound sharp as a firecracker in the dark room. Her roommate Lin Mei didn't stir. She never did. Could sleep through an earthquake, that one.

Xia grabbed the phone, squinting at the screen.

*Welcome to Samsara.*

*Your first past life has been unlocked.*

*Initiating transfer in 10 seconds.*

Her thumb hovered over the notification. She should delete it. This was obviously some kind of virus, some dark web scam that would steal her banking info or turn her phone into a botnet.

But she didn't delete it.

She'd downloaded the app three hours ago, after following a breadcrumb trail of forum posts. A user called *GhostOfChang'an* had left a comment on a history forum: *"The past isn't memory. It's a place you can visit."* The other users had laughed at him. Called him a LARPing weirdo. But Xia had clicked his profile, found a link to a Telegram channel, then another link, then another.

A digital rabbit hole that ended with an APK file.

She'd installed it on impulse. Told herself she'd uninstall it in the morning.

Now the countdown was ticking.

*5 seconds.*

She should close the app. Should put the phone down and go back to sleep like a normal person.

*3 seconds.*

The screen went white.

Then black.

Then nothing.

---

Zhang Xia opened her eyes.

She was standing in a courtyard. Ancient trees rose around her, their branches heavy with snow. The air smelled of pine and woodsmoke and something else—something she couldn't name but recognized anyway. Incense. Burning paper. The smell of prayer.

She looked down at her hands.

They weren't her hands.

These hands were larger, calloused along the palms, with ink stains on the fingers. A man's hands. A scholar's hands.

*What the hell.*

Her heart hammered against her ribs. No—not her ribs. Someone else's ribs. She could feel the shape of this body like a borrowed coat. Too big in the shoulders. Too long in the legs. She took a step and nearly fell, her balance all wrong.

"You coming or what?"

The voice came from behind her. Xia turned—slowly, carefully—and saw a man in Tang Dynasty robes, his face half-hidden by the shadow of his hat.

"The Emperor's waiting," the man said. "And you know how he gets when he's kept waiting."

Xia opened her mouth. A question formed on her lips—*Who are you? Where am I? What is happening?*—but what came out was:

"Let him wait. The poem won't write itself."

The voice was deep. Masculine. Confident in a way Xia had never been.

The man laughed. "There he is. The great Li Bai, humble as ever."

*Li Bai.*

The name hit Xia like a physical blow.

She knew that name. Everyone knew that name. Li Bai was the most famous poet of the Tang Dynasty, a drunkard and a genius who wrote verses that had survived twelve hundred years. Schoolchildren memorized his poems. Calligraphers copied his characters. He was a ghost, a legend, a figure carved from marble and myth.

And apparently, he was a past life of Zhang Xia.

The revelation should have been impossible. Should have sent her spiraling into denial. But as she stood there in the snow-covered courtyard, wearing Li Bai's body like a second skin, she felt something unexpected.

*This fits.*

The thought came from somewhere deep, somewhere primitive. She'd always felt out of place in her own life—a history major who couldn't stop dreaming about the past, a scholarship student who didn't belong in Beijing's elite circles, a girl whose mother had died when she was twelve and whose father had disappeared into his grief. She'd always felt like she was waiting for something.

Maybe she'd been waiting for this.

"Fine," the man said. "But if the Emperor drinks all the good wine before you get there, don't come crying to me."

He walked away, his footsteps crunching in the snow.

Xia took a breath. Li Bai's breath. The air was cold and clean and tasted like possibility.

She followed the man.

---

The palace was everything she'd imagined and nothing like it.

The history books described Tang Dynasty architecture in careful academic language—*curved eaves, vermilion pillars, cosmic symmetry*—but the books didn't capture the smell. The smoke from a thousand oil lamps. The sweat of courtiers who'd been standing for hours. The faint sweetness of plum wine.

They didn't capture the weight of it either. The sheer *presence* of the Emperor, sitting on his throne like a mountain in silk robes.

Xia knelt with the other courtiers, her head bowed. She could feel the Emperor's eyes on her.

"Li Bai," the Emperor said. "I hear you've been drinking again."

She felt the words rising in her throat. Not her words. Li Bai's words. "I've been drinking, yes. But I've also been writing."

"You're always writing. The question is whether you've written anything worth reading."

A ripple of laughter through the court. Xia felt the sting of it—felt it in Li Bai's bones, in the way his jaw tightened and his fingers curled into fists.

She opened her mouth to respond—

And the world shattered.

---

She was back in her dorm room.

The ceiling fan spun overhead, clicking with each rotation. Her phone lay on the pillow beside her, screen dark. 3:58 AM. Eleven minutes had passed.

Xia sat up, her heart pounding. Her hands were her hands again. Small. Thin. The calluses from writing papers, not poetry.

She looked at her phone. The Samsara app was still open.

*Past life transfer complete.*

*Duration: 11 minutes, 23 seconds.*

*Memory retention: 87%.*

*Next transfer available in 23 hours, 47 minutes.*

She stared at the screen. Her hands were shaking.

*It was real.*

She knew it was real. She could still feel the cold air on Li Bai's face. Could still smell the incense. Could still hear the Emperor's voice, dismissive and sharp.

She could still feel the rage.

That last moment in the palace. The flash of heat in Li Bai's chest. She'd been about to say something, to defend herself, to prove herself—

*Someone had pulled her out.*

Not a glitch. Not a system error. Someone had ended the transfer early.

Xia opened the app again, scrolling through the interface. It was surprisingly simple. A timeline stretching back thousands of years, with dots marking past lives. She was at the Tang Dynasty now, a single dot glowing gold.

She tapped it.

A message appeared:

*Warning: Low compatibility with host body. Extended exposure may cause dissociation.*

*Recommended wait time: 48 hours before next transfer.*

*Note: Your transfer was interrupted by an external user.*

*User ID: GhostOfChang'an.*

Xia's blood went cold.

*GhostOfChang'an.*

The same user who'd left that comment on the forum. The one who'd led her down the rabbit hole. He'd been watching her. Waiting for her to download the app. Waiting for her to jump.

He'd pulled her out of Li Bai's body.

The question was why.

She typed a message into the app's chat function:

*Who are you?*

The response came instantly.

*GhostOfChang'an: Someone who's been where you are.*

*GhostOfChang'an: And someone who needs your help.*

Xia's thumbs hovered over the keyboard. This was insane. She should delete the app. Should report this to the police. Should do literally anything except continue this conversation.

But she couldn't stop thinking about that courtyard. That snow. That body that had fit her like a key in a lock.

*Zhang Xia: Why did you pull me out?*

*GhostOfChang'an: Because you were about to make a mistake.*

*GhostOfChang'an: Li Bai died in 762 AD. He drowned reaching for the moon's reflection in a river.*

*GhostOfChang'an: If you'd stayed too long, the Emperor would have sent you away. Exiled you. You would have missed the window.*

*Zhang Xia: What window?*

*GhostOfChang'an: The window you're about to use.*

*GhostOfChang'an: Tomorrow night. 2 AM. The app will offer you a second jump.*

*GhostOfChang'an: Take it.*

*Zhang Xia: Take it where?*

*GhostOfChang'an: The Warring States period. 230 BC.*

*GhostOfChang'an: There's someone you need to meet.*

The chat went silent.

Xia waited. Typed another message. Nothing.

The app had gone dark, the timeline disappearing, the dots fading until only the Tang Dynasty remained.

She lay back in her bed, staring at the ceiling.

*The past isn't memory. It's a place you can visit.*

She'd thought GhostOfChang'an was a conspiracy theorist. A LARPing weirdo. Someone who'd found a creative way to talk about history.

Now she wasn't so sure.

He'd known she was in the Tang Dynasty. Known exactly when to pull her out. Known what would happen if she stayed.

He was playing a game. And somehow, she was a piece on his board.

The question was whether she was a pawn or a queen.

Xia closed her eyes. The snow from the courtyard still clung to her memory. The cold. The weight of Li Bai's robes. The feeling of standing in a body that was centuries dead.

She should run. Should forget this ever happened.

But as she drifted toward sleep, she found herself thinking about the Warring States period. 230 BC. The final years before Qin Shi Huang unified China. A time of chaos and blood and ambition.

A time when the world was being rewritten.

*There's someone you need to meet.*

She didn't know who. Didn't know why. Didn't know what GhostOfChang'an wanted from her.

But she knew one thing with absolute certainty.

She was going to take that jump.

And she was going to find out what the hell was going on.

Her phone buzzed one last time.

*GhostOfChang'an: One more thing.*

*GhostOfChang'an: Don't trust anyone.*

The message disappeared before she could reply.

And Zhang Xia was alone in the dark, counting down the hours until she could become someone else.

title: Chapter 3
content:
CHAPTER 3: THE POET WHO NEVER DIED

The notification came at 3:47 AM.

Zhang Xia's phone vibrated against the wooden desk in her dorm room, the screen glowing through the darkness. She'd been staring at the Samsara app icon for hours, unable to sleep, unable to look away. Her thumb hovered over it, trembling.

*One new life available.*

She clicked.

The world dissolved.

---

She woke on her knees.

Cold stone pressed through thin silk robes. Incense smoke curled around her, thick and sweet, burning her throat. Candles flickered in bronze holders, casting dancing shadows across walls covered in calligraphy. Not the simplified characters she'd learned in school. Ancient script. Elegant. Flowing like water.

A woman knelt beside her. Older. Gray streaking her hair, face lined with something between worry and reverence. She wore layers of blue silk, a jade hairpin holding her bun in place.

"Mistress," the woman whispered. "The summons came from the palace. You must prepare."

Zhang Xia tried to speak. Her voice came out wrong. Deeper. More refined. "The palace?"

"His Majesty requests your presence at the evening banquet." The woman's hands moved quickly, adjusting Zhang Xia's robes, straightening the collar. "He's heard of your latest poem. The one about the moon."

*Poem. Moon. Palace.*

The memories crashed into her like a wave.

Li Qingzhao. No. Not her. Someone else. A poet from the Tang Dynasty. Name rising from the fog of her borrowed mind. Zhang Ruoxu. That was her name now. A scholar. A woman of letters. Famous for a single poem that would survive a thousand years.

Would survive. Not had.

The present tense burned in her chest.

"I don't understand," Zhang Xia said, but the words came out as Zhang Ruoxu. "I was just in my dorm. I was—"

"Mistress, you're speaking nonsense again." The servant's hands paused. Worry flickered across her face. "The fever dreams. They've returned?"

*Fever dreams.*

Zhang Xia looked down at her hands. Pale. Slender. Ink stains on the fingers. Real. Solid. *This* was real. The dorm room, the app, the twenty-first century — that was the dream.

Wasn't it?

She pressed her palm against the stone floor. Cold. Rough. A thousand years before concrete and steel. Before electricity. Before anything she knew.

The servant helped her stand. Bronze mirrors lined the wall, and Zhang Xia caught her reflection. A stranger's face stared back. High cheekbones. Dark eyes. Hair piled in elaborate knots. Beautiful in a way Zhang Xia had never been.

But the eyes were the same. Frightened. Confused. Searching.

---

The palace complex sprawled across the capital like a living thing.

Zhang Xia walked through corridors that seemed to stretch forever, her silk robes whispering against the ground. Guards stood at every doorway, their armor gleaming in torchlight. Servants bowed as she passed. They knew her. Respected her.

*Zhang Ruoxu. Poet of the court. Friend to ministers. Confidante of emperors.*

The memories kept coming, fragments of a life she hadn't lived. A childhood by the river. A father who taught her to write. A husband who died young. Years of loneliness filled with ink and paper and the desperate need to leave something behind.

Something beautiful. Something permanent.

"Lady Zhang." A eunuch appeared at her elbow, his voice high and smooth. "His Majesty awaits."

She followed him into the banquet hall.

Hundreds of candles lit the space. Tables lined the walls, laden with dishes she couldn't name. Officials in silk robes sat cross-legged, drinking wine, talking in low voices. Musicians played in the corner, strings and flutes weaving melodies she'd heard before. In another life.

At the center, on a raised dais, sat the Emperor.

He looked younger than she expected. Mid-thirties. Strong jaw. Eyes that missed nothing. He watched her enter, and the conversations around them died.

"Zhang Ruoxu," he said. "Come. Sit beside me."

She walked forward, her heart hammering. The other officials watched with envy and curiosity. She was nobody. A woman. A poet. Why did the Emperor favor her?

Because of the poem.

The knowledge surfaced like a fish breaking water. The poem she'd written last week. About the river. About the moon reflecting on the water. About loneliness and eternity.

*"The moon lingers above the river's face,  
Watching the generations flow beneath.  
We who live and die are grains of sand,  
But the moon remembers every name."*

The Emperor recited the lines as she sat down. His voice carried across the hall.

"A beautiful poem," he said. "But I wonder. What did you mean by 'the moon remembers'?"

Zhang Xia's throat went dry.

The room felt smaller. The candles flickered. She could feel the weight of history pressing down on her, the knowledge of what was to come. This dynasty would fall. The capital would burn. The Emperor would die, killed by rebels in a coup that would be written about for centuries.

And somewhere, in the future, a girl named Zhang Xia would read about it in a textbook.

"I meant," she said slowly, "that the moon has witnessed everything. It watched our ancestors. It will watch our descendants. We are brief. It is eternal."

The Emperor nodded. "But you said it *remembers*. As if it chooses to hold onto us."

*Because it does.*

The thought came unbidden. Not hers. Zhang Ruoxu's. The poet believed something. Something she'd never told anyone.

"I have a question for Your Majesty," Zhang Xia said.

The Emperor raised an eyebrow. "Speak."

"Do you believe in past lives?"

Silence crashed through the hall. A cup dropped somewhere. Wine splattered across the floor.

The Emperor's face went still. Carefully, deliberately still. "Why do you ask?"

"Because I've been having dreams," Zhang Xia said. The words poured out before she could stop them. "Dreams of another time. Another body. A life that isn't mine. And I know it's real. I know I've lived before."

The Emperor stared at her for a long moment.

Then he laughed.

"Lady Zhang, you amuse me." He raised his cup. "To poets and their fancies. May they never stop dreaming."

The officials laughed with him. The tension broke. Music resumed. Conversations started again.

But Zhang Xia saw something in the Emperor's eyes before he turned away.

Fear.

---

The banquet lasted four hours.

Zhang Xia ate food she couldn't taste. Drank wine that burned her throat. Made conversation with ministers who quoted her poetry back to her. All the while, her mind raced.

*The app. This is the app. I'm living a past life.*

But it felt too real. The ache in her knees from sitting cross-legged. The weight of the silk. The smell of incense and wine and sweat. This wasn't a memory. This was *now*.

And somewhere, in another time, her body lay in a dorm room, hooked up to a phone that had no business existing.

When the banquet finally ended, the Emperor dismissed everyone except her.

"Stay," he said. "I want to show you something."

He led her through a hidden door behind his throne. A narrow staircase spiraled downward, torchlight flickering on stone walls. The air grew cold. Damp. They descended for what felt like minutes.

At the bottom, a room.

No windows. No furniture. Just walls covered in paintings. Murals stretching from floor to ceiling, depicting scenes she recognized. Battles. Coronations. Deaths. Births.

And in every scene, the same figure.

A woman. Standing at the edges. Watching.

The Emperor pointed. "Do you know who that is?"

Zhang Xia shook her head.

"I've been having dreams too," he said quietly. "Dreams of a woman who appears throughout history. In every dynasty. Every era. She's always there. Always watching." He turned to face her. "Last week, I dreamed she spoke to me. She said my time was ending. That a new age was coming."

"That's just a dream," Zhang Xia whispered.

"Is it?" The Emperor's hand touched the wall. "These paintings have been here for centuries. Hidden. My father showed them to me. His father showed him. The woman in these paintings — she's real. She walks through time."

Zhang Xia's blood turned cold.

"Who is she?"

"I don't know." The Emperor's eyes met hers. "But in my dream, she had your face."

---

The world shattered.

Zhang Xia gasped, her body jerking upright. The dorm room spun around her. Fluorescent lights. Posters on the wall. Her roommate's snoring from the bottom bunk.

The phone was still in her hand. The Samsara app still open.

But something had changed.

A new message notification glowed at the bottom of the screen.

*Warning: Another user has been detected in your timeline.*

*Identity: Unknown.*

*Status: Active.*

*Last known life: Tang Dynasty, 718 AD.*

Zhang Xia stared at the words.

*The Emperor.*

The Emperor had been using the app too.

But that was impossible. The Tang Dynasty ended over a thousand years ago. How could someone from that time have access to a modern app?

Unless the app wasn't modern.

Unless it had always existed.

Her phone buzzed again. Another notification.

*New life unlocked.*

*Your next jump begins in five seconds.*

"No," Zhang Xia said. "No, I need to—"

The world dissolved.

She woke on a battlefield.

Iron swords clashed around her. Horses screamed. Blood soaked the ground, turning mud to paste. Men in armor fought and died, their faces young, terrified. A banner waved in the distance, characters she recognized.

*Warring States period.*

A hand grabbed her shoulder. A voice shouted in her ear. "General! The enemy is flanking us! What are your orders?"

Zhang Xia looked down at herself.

Armor. Blood. A sword in her hand.

And in her mind, a voice that wasn't hers whispered:

*You've been here before.*

*You've always been here.*

*And so has she.*

title: The Stranger Who Knew Her Name
content:
The noodle shop smelled of soy and star anise. Zhang Xia sat in the corner booth, her phone face-down on the sticky table. She hadn't touched her bowl. The noodles had gone cold twenty minutes ago.

She kept replaying it. The Tang Dynasty marketplace. The woman bleeding in the dirt. The sword. The way the world had dissolved into code, ones and zeros bleeding through the sky like digital rain.

"Not hungry?"

Zhang Xia looked up. A man stood at her table. Middle-aged, maybe forty-five. Gray streaked his temples. He wore a plain gray jacket, the kind a teacher might wear. Nothing special. But his eyes—dark, patient, watching her like he already knew what she would say next.

"Who are you?"

He pulled out the chair across from her. Sat down. The movement was careful, deliberate. Like every gesture was calculated.

"You downloaded the app," he said. "Three days ago. 2:47 AM. You found it through a link on a forum about lucid dreaming."

Zhang Xia's throat tightened. "How do you—"

"I know because I've been waiting for someone to find it." He signaled the shop owner. "Two bowls of beef noodles. Fresh ones."

The owner grunted and shuffled toward the kitchen. Zhang Xia stared at the man. Her fingers itched toward her phone.

"Don't call anyone," he said. "And don't open the app again. Not until we talk."

"You're talking like you know what's happening to me."

"I do." He leaned forward. "My name is Chen Wei. I was a researcher at Tsinghua University. Computational neuroscience. Memory encoding. The architecture of consciousness."

"And that relates to this how?"

Chen Wei smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "Five years ago, a student came to me. She said she'd been having dreams. Not normal dreams. She described them as 'continuous'—like she was living a second life while she slept. She gave me details about the Tang Dynasty that no amateur historian would know. Specific dates. Names of minor officials. The texture of the silk in a particular year."

The noodle bowls arrived. Steam rose between them. Zhang Xia didn't move.

"I thought she was mentally ill," Chen Wei continued. "I recommended a psychiatrist. She went. The psychiatrist put her on medication. The dreams stopped." He picked up his chopsticks. "She killed herself three months later."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I found her journal afterward. She wrote that the dreams weren't dreams. They were memories. But not her memories." He separated his chopsticks with a sharp snap. "She wrote that someone was using her. Living through her. And when she tried to stop it, they locked her out."

Zhang Xia's hands were shaking. She pressed them flat against the table. "The app. It's not just... reliving past lives."

"No." Chen Wei dipped his noodles into the broth. "The app is a bridge. A two-way bridge. When you enter a past life through Samsara, you're not just observing. You're occupying. And whoever created the app can occupy too."

"That's impossible."

"Is it?" He looked at her. "You saw the woman die. In the marketplace. You saw her blood. You felt her fear. That was real. That happened. And the woman who killed her—"

"Was a man. In modern clothes."

Chen Wei's chopsticks stopped moving. "You saw that."

"I saw his face. Young. Late twenties. Black jacket. He looked..." She searched for the word. "Purposeful."

"Did he see you?"

Zhang Xia remembered. The moment before everything shattered. The stranger's eyes lifting. Finding her across the crowd. The way he'd smiled, like he'd been expecting her.

"He saw me."

Chen Wei set down his chopsticks. The sound was loud in the quiet shop.

"Then he knows who you are now. Which means we don't have much time."

"Time for what?"

"For you to understand what you've become." He reached into his jacket and pulled out a phone. Different from hers. Older. The screen was cracked. "I've been tracking the app's source code for three years. It's not standard programming. It's built on something I've never seen before. A language that doesn't follow normal logic. It's like someone built software that runs on consciousness itself."

"Who built it?"

"We don't know. But we know what it does." He turned the phone toward her. On the screen, a diagram. Circles within circles. "Every time someone uses Samsara, they create a connection to a past life. But the connection isn't static. It's a thread. And threads can be pulled."

"Pulled where?"

"To the present." He met her eyes. "Whoever controls the app can rewrite history by sending changes backward. Small changes. A merchant who lives instead of dies. A letter that gets delivered instead of lost. A decision made differently. Each change ripples forward."

Zhang Xia's mind raced. "But if they change the past, wouldn't they change themselves? Paradox?"

"Unless they're outside the timeline." Chen Wei's voice dropped. "Unless they've been alive for so long that they've learned to navigate the currents instead of drowning in them."

"You're saying the person behind this is immortal."

"I'm saying they've been using Samsara to jump between bodies for centuries. Each time they die, they find a new vessel. A new life. They keep their memories. Their knowledge. Their purpose."

"What purpose?"

Chen Wei was about to answer when his phone buzzed. He looked down. His face went pale.

"They found us."

Zhang Xia turned. Through the shop's window, she saw them. Three people in black coats, standing across the street. They weren't moving. Just watching. Their faces blank.

"Who are they?"

"Collectors." Chen Wei stood. "People the app has claimed. Their consciousness has been overwritten. They're shells now. Controlled by whoever holds the master thread."

"Overwritten?" Zhang Xia's voice cracked. "You mean their minds are—"

"Gone. Replaced." He grabbed her arm. "We need to leave. Now."

They moved toward the back of the shop. The owner shouted something. Chen Wei threw money on the counter. They burst through the kitchen door into an alley.

The alley was empty. Garbage cans. A stray cat. Fire escapes zigzagging up brick walls.

"This way." Chen Wei pulled her toward a metal ladder. "Climb."

Zhang Xia climbed. Her hands slipped on the cold metal. Above her, the sky was gray, bruised with clouds. Behind her, she heard footsteps. Slow. Unhurried.

She reached the roof. Chen Wei was already there, pulling her up. They ran across the gravel, past air conditioning units, toward the edge of the building.

"There." He pointed. A gap between buildings. Three feet. Maybe four. "Jump."

"You're insane."

"Jump, or they take you. And if they take you, you won't be you anymore."

She looked back. The Collectors were climbing the fire escape. Their movements were synchronized. Mechanical.

Zhang Xia jumped.

She hit the other roof hard. Pain shot through her ankle. She rolled, scrambled up. Chen Wei landed beside her, grunting.

"They'll keep coming," he said. "Until we lose them in the system."

"System?"

He pulled out his phone again. "The app runs on a central server. A physical location. I found it three weeks ago. An old factory in the industrial district." He typed rapidly. "I've been building a counter-app. Something that can sever the connection. But I need to upload it from the source."

"You want to go to their base?"

"I want to end this." He looked at her. "But I can't do it alone. I need someone who's already connected. Someone whose thread is still active."

"You need me to use the app again."

"No." His voice was hard. "I need you to trust me. When we get to the factory, you'll enter Samsara one more time. But this time, you won't go where the app takes you. You'll go where I send you."

"Where?"

"To the moment the app was created." He held her gaze. "Someone built Samsara. Someone wrote its first line of code. I need you to find them and stop them before they finish."

Zhang Xia stared at him. The wind whipped her hair across her face. Below, the Collectors had reached the roof. They stood in a line, watching.

"I don't even know how to control where I go," she said.

"You'll learn." Chen Wei's phone chimed. "Or you'll be trapped forever in someone else's life."

He turned and ran. Zhang Xia followed.

Behind them, the Collectors began to move.

title: The Poet's Last Breath
content:
The notification pinged at 3:47 AM.

Zhang Xia's phone lit up against the ceiling of her dorm room. She'd been lying awake for hours, replaying the Tang Dynasty in fragments—the taste of wine, the weight of silk, the ache in a dead man's bones.

She grabbed the phone.

*Warning: Samsara has detected unauthorized timeline interference. A past-life anchor has been disrupted. If your current incarnation is linked to this anchor, you may experience—*

The message cut off.

Then the world flipped.

---

She was standing in a rain-soaked courtyard, her body wrong. Too tall. Too thin. Hands that weren't hers gripped a brush, trembling over a scroll of yellowed paper.

*No. Not again. Not without the app.*

But this wasn't the smooth transition of a sanctioned jump. This was a *pull*—something yanking her through centuries like a fish on a hook.

The courtyard resolved around her. Bamboo groaned in the wind. Lanterns swung, their flames sputtering. A woman's voice cut through the rain, low and desperate.

"You cannot finish it."

Zhang Xia turned. The woman stood beneath the eaves, her face half-lit by a dying lantern. She wore the robes of a Tang courtesan, but her eyes held something colder than any pleasure house—*recognition*.

"You're not him," the woman said. "You're the jumper."

"I don't know what you—"

"Don't." The woman stepped forward, rain soaking her sleeves. "I've been watching through his eyes for three years. You're the one who's been breaking the threads."

Zhang Xia's blood went cold. "You're another user."

"I'm the one trying to stop the *bastard* who's been rewriting my husband's death for a thousand years." The woman's voice cracked. "And you just helped him."

The scroll in Zhang Xia's hands. She looked down.

The calligraphy was flawless. Characters formed with a ghost's precision, each stroke carrying the weight of a dying man's final act. Li Bai's last poem. The one history said was lost.

"I didn't write this," Zhang Xia whispered.

"Of course you didn't. *He* did. Through you." The woman grabbed her wrist, nails biting. "You think Samsara is just an app? It's a *door*. And someone's been leaving it open, letting the dead walk through. Every time you jump, you're a vessel. But the poison isn't you—it's the person who *sent* you here."

The rain stopped. Silence fell like a blade.

"The man in the white suit," Zhang Xia said. "He gave me the app."

The courtesan's face went pale. "Impossible. He's been dead for sixty years."

"Who is he?"

"He's the first jumper. The one who figured out you don't just *live* past lives—you can *steal* them. He's been collecting deaths like currency. Every life he takes, he adds to his own." Her grip tightened. "And Li Bai's death is worth more than you know."

Thunder rolled, but the sky was clear.

"I have to finish the poem," Zhang Xia said.

"No. If you do, the timeline locks. Li Bai drowns reaching for the moon, just like the legends say. But that's not how it happened. That's the *lie* he wrote into history."

"Then what really happened?"

The courtesan's eyes filled with tears. "He was murdered. By the man in the white suit. And his death was supposed to be a *warning*—a poet's life means nothing to those who hold the brush of heaven. If you complete the poem, you seal the lie. You let him win."

Zhang Xia looked at the scroll. The characters seemed to pulse, alive with trapped meaning.

*I reach for the moon's reflection—*

She lifted the brush.

"Don't." The woman's voice broke. "I loved him. I've been watching him die for thirty lifetimes. Please."

"You said I was a vessel," Zhang Xia said. "But vessels can be filled with different things."

She dipped the brush in ink.

And wrote a different ending.

---

The world lurched.

The courtyard dissolved into static, colors bleeding like wet paint. Zhang Xia felt herself falling not through space, but through *time*—centuries flickering past like pages in a burning book.

She saw Li Bai, not drowning, but standing on a mountain peak, laughing at the moon.

She saw the man in the white suit, younger, his hands covered in something dark.

She saw a woman who looked exactly like her, standing in a modern high-rise, holding a phone identical to her own.

*You're not the first jumper,* a voice whispered. *You're not even the hundredth.*

Then Zhang Xia hit concrete.

---

She was back in her dorm room. 3:49 AM. Two minutes had passed.

Her phone was dead. Black screen. No amount of button-holding brought it back.

But there was something in her hand.

A scroll. Yellowed. Tied with red silk.

She unrolled it.

The characters were different from before. Smaller. Written in a woman's hand.

*To the girl who borrowed my lover's skin:*

*He is watching you now. Not through the app—through the lives you haven't lived yet. Every death you avoid becomes a debt you owe. Every choice you change, a thread he can pull.*

*The poet's death is still broken. You didn't fix it; you just wrote a new lie.*

*But I know how to stop him.*

*Find the woman who walks backward through time. She is the only one who remembers the original world.*

*—Lian*

Zhang Xia's hands shook.

Footsteps in the hallway. Slow. Deliberate. Coming toward her door.

She grabbed her phone. Dead. The door had no lock, just a flimsy handle that turned with a twist.

The footsteps stopped.

A silver light slid under the crack of the door.

And a voice, smooth as silk, said:

"You changed the poem, Zhang Xia. That was rude."

She backed against the wall. The scroll crumpled in her fist.

"Don't bother running," the voice continued. "I've been editing history for six centuries. I know how every version of you ends."

The handle began to turn.

Zhang Xia looked at the scroll. At the last line.

*She is the only one who remembers the original world.*

She didn't know what it meant. Didn't know who to trust.

But the handle was turning, and she had nothing but a dead phone and a dead poet's final lie.

She chose.

She kicked the window open and jumped.

---

Three floors. Concrete below. Rain on her face.

But she didn't hit the ground.

She hit *something else*—a cold, liquid darkness that swallowed her whole.

And when she opened her eyes, she was standing in a room made of mirrors.

In every reflection, a different version of her stared back.

A Tang poet.

A Warring States soldier.

A Qing rebel with a sword at her throat.

And in the mirror at the far end, the man in the white suit smiled.

"Welcome to the Samsara Archive," he said. "The place where every life you've ever lived is stored."

He raised a hand.

"Let me show you how many endings I've written for you."

The mirrors shattered.

Zhang Xia screamed.

And the last thing she saw before the darkness took her was a woman walking toward her—backward, her feet pointing the wrong way, her eyes fixed on something behind Zhang Xia's head.

The woman who walks backward through time.

She was already too late.
